answers.styleguides.existing_conventions: Generate fresh guides — don't incorporate the repo's existing ruff/mypy config specifics.
answers.workflow.tdd_strictness: Moderate — tests encouraged, not blocking, especially where a project already has good coverage.
answers.workflow.code_review: Optional / self-review OK — this is a solo-owned repo, but let bot reviewers (Kilo, CodeRabbit, CodeAnt, Gemini Code Assist) run and address their findings before merging.
answers.workflow.verification_checkpoints: Only at track completion — automated tests run per task/phase as work happens, but a full manual verification pass is reserved for when the whole track is done.
answers.tech_stack.infrastructure: Modal is the primary compute/deploy target for now. Other infrastructure pieces (e.g. GitHub Actions for CI/labels, possibly Cloudflare) will likely be added later, but Modal is the focus while the core apps stabilize.
answers.tech_stack.languages: Python (FastAPI/Modal apps: modal-runner, modal-agy, modal-sandbox, modal-images) and Rust (sovereign_engine pyo3 crate in modal-opencode/engine) today, plus Bash for setup/orchestrator scripts. Planned: a Rust CLI + desktop app, with a bun/npm wrapper so it's also runnable via npm or bun.
answers.tech_stack.frontend: None yet — CLI-only for now. Planned: Tauri v2 + a web frontend for the desktop app, once the CLI and API are stable.
answers.tech_stack.backend: FastAPI on Modal (modal-runner, modal-sandbox, modal-images/search_service.py), served via modal.asgi_app() / modal.fastapi_endpoint(). Hard constraint: client-side tooling (CLI, future desktop app) must run on every OS the user works from, including Termux on Android — avoid platform-specific assumptions in tooling code.
answers.tech_stack.database: Moving to an adapter pattern with three swappable layers: vector store adapter (currently FAISS in-memory), DB adapter (currently CrateDB via asyncpg), and storage adapter. Default implementation runs inside the sandbox/container; swap out a layer later only if that becomes too costly/wasteful.
answers.product_guidelines.voice_tone: Casual and friendly, primarily in Thai — this is a personal repo first, so documentation should read like notes to oneself, not a public-facing product.
product_guidelines.design_principles: Simplicity — reuse existing image tags, rebuild only when the base toolchain/model/deps actually change
Cost-awareness — Modal compute costs money per run; don't test iteratively on Modal, get it right locally first
Developer experience for the agents using this repo
Flexible, organized architecture over rigid structure
Control cost appropriately per job/workload, not one-size-fits-all
answers.product.description: A personal monorepo of Modal-hosted apps for AI-agent infrastructure: webhook task tracking (modal-runner), agent sandbox orchestration (modal-agy), a general-purpose sandbox API (modal-sandbox), and a Rust/Node dev image + embedding/reranker search service (modal-images).
answers.product.problem_statement: Local machines can't give a CLI agent and its human user a shared sandbox/workspace to do the same work together, and local RAM/CPU is a hard ceiling for agent workloads. Running agent tooling (sandboxes, dev containers, search) on Modal's serverless model instead means elastic compute, no idle cost, and a workspace reachable from anywhere by both the agent and the user.
answers.product.target_users: Solo developer and small team members who run AI coding agents (Claude Code, agy/Antigravity, opencode) that need shared sandboxed compute and a search-augmented memory index alongside the human collaborators using the same tooling.
product.key_goals: Validate the stack with real-world usage: the human user, Claude, and Hermes all working against it.
Hermes (and other agents) can run their backend workloads on Modal reliably.
